title: Swinney would 'consider' banning US military planes from Prestwick Airport

sections.heading: What happened
sections.content: Swinney would 'consider' US military plane ban from Prestwick Airport A number of US military planes have been seen at Prestwick Airport since the start of the action against Iran First Minister John Swinney has said he would consider banning US military aircraft from using state-owned Prestwick Airport if it was confirmed they were involved in strikes in the Middle East. Swinney has previously said he was "concerned" by the US and Israeli action against Iran and did not believe it conformed with an "international rules-based system".

sections.heading: Key details
sections.content: He told the BBC's Laura Kuenssberg he was seeking clarity from the UK government about the role of US planes using the airport. Pressed on a possible ban, he said: "I will consider that based on the evidence that's provided to me." The Ministry of Defence said the airport was not being used to launch military strikes. Tehran sees strikes 'every few hours', residents tell BBC as Trump demands Iran's 'unconditional surrender'

sections.heading: Why it matters
sections.content: An MoD spokesperson said: "Prestwick is a civilian International airport owned by the Scottish government and is not being used by the US to launch military strikes.

sourceReferences.citationText: serper-scottish-aviation – US military plane takes off from Prestwick Airport
